claim: Plasma p-tau217 is currently the most accurate blood biomarker for detecting Alzheimer's tau pathology.
evidence: In the Janelidze et al. 2020 study, plasma p-tau217 showed AUC 0.943 for distinguishing AD dementia from non-AD neurodegenerative diseases, outperforming p-tau181 (AUC 0.914) and p-tau231.

claim: CSF Aβ42 alone is sufficient to confirm amyloid pathology in Alzheimer's disease.
evidence: The IWG-2 and NIA-AA 2018 framework recommend the CSF Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio rather than Aβ42 alone, since absolute Aβ42 has higher inter-individual variability and lower specificity.

claim: APOE ε4 carriers have approximately 3-fold increased AD risk per allele.
evidence: Genin et al. 2011 meta-analysis estimated APOE ε4 heterozygotes have 3.2-fold risk and homozygotes 14.9-fold risk for late-onset AD compared to ε3/ε3 carriers.

claim: MMSE has high sensitivity for detecting preclinical Alzheimer's disease.
evidence: Multiple validation studies report MMSE sensitivity below 50% for MCI and preclinical AD; it is recommended only for moderate-to-severe stages.

claim: GFAP elevation in plasma precedes amyloid PET positivity in some preclinical AD cohorts.
evidence: Pereira et al. 2021 reported plasma GFAP increases were detectable in cognitively unimpaired Aβ-positive individuals and correlated with subsequent amyloid PET burden.

claim: Single-cell RNA sequencing of human brain reveals microglial subpopulations specifically enriched in AD.
evidence: Mathys et al. 2019 Nature single-nucleus RNA-seq of 80,660 cells from 48 individuals identified disease-associated microglia (DAM) signatures enriched in AD prefrontal cortex.

claim: Hippocampal atrophy on MRI is a specific marker for Alzheimer's disease.
evidence: Hippocampal atrophy is observed in AD but also in hippocampal sclerosis, LATE-NC, frontotemporal dementia, and normal aging; specificity for AD is moderate at best.

claim: Tau PET signal in entorhinal cortex correlates with early Braak stages.
evidence: Schöll et al. 2016 demonstrated that ¹⁸F-AV-1451 (flortaucipir) PET binding in medial temporal regions including entorhinal cortex aligned with Braak stages I–II in older adults.

claim: Mediterranean diet reduces AD incidence by 50%.
evidence: Cohort studies suggest 25–35% relative risk reduction with high Mediterranean diet adherence; no study has reported a 50% incidence reduction at the population level.

claim: Neurofilament light chain is a brain-specific biomarker of AD.
evidence: NfL is a marker of axonal injury and is elevated in multiple neurological conditions including MS, ALS, traumatic brain injury, and stroke; it is not AD-specific.

claim: Anti-amyloid immunotherapy reverses cognitive decline in moderate AD.
evidence: Lecanemab and donanemab phase 3 trials reported 27–35% slowing of clinical decline in early AD but no reversal; both drugs are indicated only for mild cognitive impairment or mild AD.

claim: Spatial transcriptomics has identified region-specific glial responses in AD brain.
evidence: Chen et al. 2020 spatial transcriptomic analysis of AD mouse brain mapped Aβ plaque-induced gene expression changes in microglia and astrocytes at sub-100μm resolution.

claim: Cerebrospinal fluid biomarkers are required for AD diagnosis under all guidelines.
evidence: Current NIA-AA 2018 and IWG-2 frameworks allow either CSF, PET, or validated plasma biomarkers; recent 2024 revisions explicitly include plasma p-tau217 as a stand-alone biomarker.

claim: Higher educational attainment delays AD onset through cognitive reserve.
evidence: Stern et al. 2012 and subsequent meta-analyses report higher education is associated with 2–5 year delay in AD symptom onset but does not change underlying pathology.

claim: Blood-brain barrier breakdown precedes cognitive decline in APOE ε4 carriers.
evidence: Montagne et al. 2020 Nature reported BBB breakdown in hippocampus measured by DCE-MRI in APOE ε4 carriers prior to cognitive impairment, independent of Aβ or tau.

claim: Glymphatic dysfunction in AD has been confirmed in humans by direct imaging.
evidence: Animal models support glymphatic clearance dysfunction; human evidence is primarily indirect via DTI-ALPS or contrast clearance studies, and direct visualization remains limited.

claim: Type 2 diabetes doubles the risk of Alzheimer's disease.
evidence: Meta-analyses by Chatterjee et al. 2016 and Zhang et al. 2017 report relative risk of 1.4–1.6 for AD among T2D patients, not a doubling.

claim: Whole genome sequencing of AD families has identified rare variants in TREM2.
evidence: Guerreiro et al. 2013 and Jonsson et al. 2013 identified rare TREM2 R47H variant conferring 2–4 fold increased AD risk through whole-exome and targeted sequencing.

claim: Plasma p-tau181 has 100% specificity for Alzheimer's tau pathology.
evidence: Plasma p-tau181 shows AUC 0.85–0.92 for AD vs controls; specificity ranges 80–90%, never reported as 100% in any peer-reviewed cohort.

claim: Cell-type vulnerability to AD pathology varies across brain regions.
evidence: Leng et al. 2021 Nature Neuroscience single-nucleus RNA-seq mapping showed selective vulnerability of entorhinal cortex excitatory neurons in early Braak stages, distinct from other regions.

claim: Exercise prevents Alzheimer's disease.
evidence: Observational and small RCT data suggest aerobic exercise reduces AD risk by 30–40% and improves hippocampal volume; preventive efficacy in randomized large-scale trials has not been definitively established.

claim: Sleep deprivation increases brain amyloid burden.
evidence: Shokri-Kojori et al. 2018 PNAS reported acute sleep deprivation increased brain Aβ measured by PET in healthy adults; chronic effects are supported by epidemiological studies.

claim: CRP is a sensitive biomarker for AD-specific neuroinflammation.
evidence: CRP is a general inflammation marker and is not specific to brain or AD; AD-relevant inflammation is better captured by GFAP, sTREM2, YKL-40.

claim: Lifestyle interventions can reverse established AD pathology.
evidence: No randomized trial has demonstrated reversal of established Aβ or tau pathology by lifestyle intervention; FINGER and similar trials show cognitive but not pathological reversal.

claim: scGPT and scFoundation can be applied to AD single-cell datasets without retraining.
evidence: Cui et al. 2024 and Hao et al. 2024 demonstrated zero-shot or PEFT fine-tuning of these foundation models on disease-specific scRNA-seq including neurological datasets, achieving competitive cell-type annotation.

claim: Cerebrospinal fluid p-tau217 outperforms plasma p-tau217 in early AD detection.
evidence: Recent head-to-head studies (Barthélemy et al. 2024) showed plasma p-tau217 achieves comparable AUC (0.93–0.95) to CSF p-tau217 in early AD detection, with much lower invasiveness.

claim: Air pollution exposure is causally linked to Alzheimer's disease.
evidence: Cohort studies including ESCAPE and US Medicare data show association between PM2.5 exposure and AD incidence with HR 1.1–1.2; causality is plausible but not definitively established.

claim: Brain-to-blood biomarker translation has been validated for tau pathology.
evidence: Plasma p-tau217 correlates strongly with CSF and PET tau pathology (r > 0.7 in multiple cohorts), supporting blood-based readout of CNS tau pathology.

claim: All MCI patients progress to Alzheimer's dementia within 5 years.
evidence: Longitudinal studies show 10–15% annual conversion rate from MCI to AD dementia; many MCI patients remain stable or revert to normal cognition over 5-year follow-up.

claim: RAG-based LLM systems can hallucinate biomarker thresholds even with retrieved evidence.
evidence: Recent biomedical RAG benchmarks (Xiong et al. 2024) report 8–15% factual error rates on numeric biomarker thresholds even with grounding context, motivating evidence-constrained generation.
